title: Prompt Engineering — Techniques, Strategies & Production Pipelines
description: At Staff level, prompt engineering is not about clever templates — it's about systematically managing the input context: choosing the right prompting strategy, enforcing structured outputs, managing context budgets, and building reliable prompt pipelines that work at scale across millions of queries.

sections.title: Prompt Engineering: Zero-Shot, Few-Shot, Chain-of-Thought
sections.description:
psychology Intuition: Different prompting strategies trade off complexity vs accuracy. Zero-shot: no examples, relies entirely on instruction following. Few-shot: provide 3-5 examples in context, the model learns the pattern from the examples in its forward pass (no weight updates). Chain-of-Thought (CoT): prompt the model to reason step-by-step before answering, effectively using more inference compute for harder problems.

bar_chart Concrete numbers: Few-shot (3-5 examples) improves accuracy 10-30% over zero-shot across classification and extraction tasks. CoT improves reasoning accuracy 15-25% on math (GSM8K) and logic benchmarks vs standard few-shot. CoT uses 3-5× more output tokens per query, increasing latency by 200-500ms at <200ms target.

track_changes Practical Workflow: Start zero-shot (cheapest). If accuracy fails, add 3-5 few-shot examples. If reasoning needed, use CoT. For complex multi-step, use self-consistency (sample N CoT paths, majority vote) — improves accuracy by 5-10% at N× cost. Meta-prompting: have the LLM analyze the task and generate its own optimal prompt — DSPy (section 3) automates this.

search Staff Engineer Question: "You have a classification task with 10 classes, 10K queries/second, <150ms p95 latency. Few-shot adds 500 tokens of context. Is it worth it?" Answer: No. 500 extra tokens at 10K QPS means 5M tokens/second additional KV cache compute. You'd need 2× more GPUs. Instead: distill the few-shot examples into a system prompt instruction, or fine-tune a small classifier model. Only use few-shot when accuracy requirements justify the infrastructure cost.

sections.title: Structured Output: JSON Mode & Constrained Decoding
sections.description:
psychology Intuition: LLMs output text — for production systems, you need structured data (JSON, XML, code). Two approaches: JSON mode (prompt the LLM to output valid JSON, then parse) vs constrained decoding (Outlines, Guidance, JSON-mode via logit masking) which forces the model to only generate tokens that produce valid structure.

bar_chart Concrete numbers: JSON mode via prompting works ~95% for simple schemas (1-level fields), drops to ~80-85% for nested schemas (3+ levels, arrays of objects). Constrained decoding (Outlines/Guidance) achieves 100% validity by construction but adds 5-10% latency due to logit masking overhead and inability to batch tokens. For mission-critical applications (automated billing, healthcare), constrained decoding is the standard. For cost-sensitive chat applications, JSON mode + retry (2 attempts) covers 99% of cases at 2% extra cost.

track_changes Practical Workflow: 1) Define your schema as Pydantic models. 2) Day 1: JSON mode + validation + retry (2 attempts). 3) Measure failure rate. If >1% for production-critical paths, add constrained decoding. 4) For high-throughput (10K+ QPS), compile constrained decoder once for the schema to amortize the one-time cost.
sections.codeBlock:
# JSON mode vs constrained decoding — production patterns
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field
from typing import List, Optional
import json, re

class ExtractSchema(BaseModel):
    entities: List[str] = Field(description="Named entities extracted")
    sentiment: str = Field(pattern="^(positive|negative|neutral)$")
    confidence: float = Field(ge=0.0, le=1.0)

def parse_json_mode(text: str, schema: type) -> Optional[BaseModel]:
    """JSON mode: prompt the model, parse, validate, retry."""
    try:
        # Strip markdown fences if present
        cleaned = re.sub(r'^```json\s*', '', text.strip(), flags=re.MULTILINE)
        cleaned = re.sub(r'\s*```$', '', cleaned)
        data = json.loads(cleaned)
        return schema(**data)
    except (json.JSONDecodeError, ValueError) as e:
        return None  # Caller retries with "Your response was not valid JSON..."

# Constrained decoding with Outlines (pseudocode)
# import outlines
# model = outlines.models.transformers("meta-llama/Meta-Llama-3-8B")
# generator = outlines.generate.json(model, ExtractSchema)
# result = generator("Extract from: Apple stock rose 3% today.")
# print(result)  # ExtractSchema(entities=["Apple"], sentiment="positive", confidence=0.95)
# ponytail: constrained decoding guarantees 100% validity but prevents
# batching — each token needs a logit mask recomputation

sections.title: DSPy: Compiling Prompt Pipelines
sections.description:
psychology Intuition: DSPy (Declarative Self-improving Python) treats prompts as optimizable program modules rather than hand-written templates. Instead of manually tweaking few-shot examples and instructions, you define a signature (input → output types), provide a metric, and DSPy compiles the optimal prompt by searching over demonstrations and instruction candidates.

bar_chart Concrete numbers: DSPy-optimized prompts match hand-crafted prompts on 80% of tasks, and exceed them on 15% (DSPy paper, 2024). For RAG pipelines, DSPy's multi-hop compilation improves accuracy 10-20% over naive RAG by finding better decomposition strategies. Compilation takes 5-30 minutes depending on task complexity and evaluator size.

track_changes Practical Workflow: Define Signature → Write a module (ChainOfThought, ReAct, etc.) → Compile with a teleprompter (BootstrapFewShot, MIPROv2) using a validation set → Deploy the compiled program. When data distribution shifts, recompile — don't hand-tune.

search Interview Angle: "How is DSPy different from AutoGPT or LangChain?" DSPy is an optimizer for prompt programs, not an agent framework. LangChain gives you building blocks; DSPy gives you a compiler that finds optimal prompts/blocks. Think of it like PyTorch vs raw CUDA — PyTorch optimizes the computation, DSPy optimizes the prompt computation.
sections.codeBlock:
# DSPy-style pipeline — RAG with compiled prompts
# pip install dspy-ai
import dspy
from dspy.teleprompt import BootstrapFewShot

class GenerateAnswer(dspy.Signature):
    """Answer questions using provided context."""
    context = dspy.InputField(desc="Relevant passages")
    question = dspy.InputField()
    answer = dspy.OutputField(desc="Factual, concise answer")

class RAG(dspy.Module):
    def __init__(self, retriever):
        super().__init__()
        self.retrieve = retriever
        self.generate = dspy.ChainOfThought(GenerateAnswer)

    def forward(self, question):
        context = self.retrieve(question, k=3)
        return self.generate(context=context, question=question)

# Compile: DSPy searches over few-shot examples + instructions
# def validate_answer(example, pred, trace=None):
#     return dspy.evaluate.answer_exact_match(example, pred)
#
# teleprompter = BootstrapFewShot(metric=validate_answer, max_bootstrapped_demos=4)
# compiled_rag = teleprompter.compile(RAG(mocked_retriever), trainset=val_set)
# compiled_rag.save("compiled_rag.json")
#
# ponytail: DSPy compilation is 5-30min; only useful if you have >500 eval examples

sections.title: The Context Window: Mechanics & Limits
sections.description:
psychology Intuition: The context window is the maximum number of tokens the model can process in a single forward pass. The fundamental constraint is attention computation: for N tokens, self-attention is O(N²) in both compute and memory. Every token attends to every other token, producing an N×N attention matrix.

bar_chart Concrete numbers: Llama 3: 128K context. Gemini 1.5 Pro: 2M context (via MoE + RingAttention). Effective context window usage drops with length due to lost-in-the-middle (section 5). Memory for KV cache at 128K: 70B model with GQA (8 KV heads) = 128K × 80 layers × 8 heads × 128 dim × 2 bytes ≈ 21 GB per sequence. With 32 concurrent sequences = 672 GB — exceeding most single-GPU memory.

track_changes Practical Workflow: Given a model with context window C: 1) Measure effective context usage (benchmark with needle-in-haystack). 2) Budget: reserve 20% of context for output, 10% for system prompt, use remaining 70% for actual content. 3) For sequences exceeding C, use sliding window or summarization. 4) Monitor per-query token usage — 10% of queries typically consume 80% of context budget.

search Interview Angle: "Your model supports 128K context but lose-in-the-middle hurts. As Staff, what do you do?" 1. Measure: run needle-in-haystack at varied depths to quantify the degradation curve. 2. Mitigate at input side: restructure documents so important info is in the first/last 25% of the context. 3. Mitigate at model side: use attention with learned positional biases (ALiBi, YaRN) or train with long-context curricula. 4. Mitigate at system side: prompt caching + summarization for documents that exceed 50K tokens. 5. Accept the remaining degradation and document the effective window (e.g., "128K nominal, 64K effective").
sections.codeBlock:
# Context window mechanics — compute and memory budgeting
import math

def attention_flops(seq_len, d_model, n_layers, n_heads):
    """Total FLOP for forward pass through all attention layers."""
    # QKV projection: N * d_model * 3*d_model
    # Attention scores: N * N * d_head * n_heads = N * N * d_model
    # Output projection: N * d_model * d_model
    attn_flops_per_layer = 4 * seq_len * d_model * d_model + 2 * seq_len * seq_len * d_model
    return n_layers * attn_flops_per_layer

def kv_cache_bytes(seq_len, n_layers, n_kv_heads, head_dim, dtype_bytes=2):
    """KV cache memory per sequence."""
    return 2 * n_layers * seq_len * n_kv_heads * head_dim * dtype_bytes

# Llama 3 8B at 128K context
flops = attention_flops(131072, 4096, 32, 32)
print(f"Attention FLOP (forward): {flops:.2e}")

kv = kv_cache_bytes(131072, 32, 8, 128)
print(f"KV cache per sequence:   {kv / 1e9:.2f} GB")
print(f"KV cache, batch=32:      {kv * 32 / 1e9:.2f} GB")

# Staff insight: at 128K context, KV cache dominates memory budget
# HBM of A100-80GB: 80GB total
# After model weights (16GB for 8B FP16): 64GB for KV cache
# 64GB / 2.1GB per sequence ≈ 30 concurrent sequences at 128K context

sections.title: Lost in the Middle: Positional Bias
sections.description:
psychology Intuition: LLMs exhibit a strong positional bias: information at the beginning and end of the context is recalled with high accuracy, but information in the middle is systematically under-attended. This is not a bug — it emerges naturally from the attention mechanism's recency bias combined with training data distributions where important information appears at document boundaries.

bar_chart Concrete numbers: Liu et al. (2023) measured: 95% accuracy when relevant info at position 0-10% of context, 75% accuracy when in the middle (40-60% of context), at 128K context length. The effect worsens with longer contexts. At 2M context, middle accuracy drops to ~50% (Gemini tech report, 2024). Key insight: attention scores in the middle are diluted — the softmax over 128K tokens naturally spreads attention thinly.

track_changes Mitigations: 1) Structure first: put important info at positions 1-5K (primacy) or last 1-2K (recency). 2) XML/JSON structured formats: delimiters like tags help attention heads localize. 3) Summarize long documents into a condensed prefix. 4) Repeat critical info: state the key fact both at the start and in a summary at the end. 5) Multi-query attention with RAG: instead of putting 100 docs in context, retrieve, answer, retrieve again.
sections.codeBlock:
# Lost-in-the-middle evaluation — needle-in-haystack test
import random, numpy as np

def needle_in_haystack(model_fn, haystack_len, needle_pos_pct, n_trials=10):
    """
    Place a specific fact (needle) at position needle_pos_pct
    in a long context (haystack). Check if model retrieves it.
    """
    haystack_text = "The capital of France is Paris. " * (haystack_len // 40)
    needle = "The secret code is 8492."
    insert_idx = int(len(haystack_text) * needle_pos_pct / 100)
    full_context = haystack_text[:insert_idx] + needle + " " + haystack_text[insert_idx:]
    query = "What is the secret code? Answer with just the number."

    correct = 0
    for _ in range(n_trials):
        response = model_fn(full_context + "\n\n" + query)
        if "8492" in response:
            correct += 1
    return correct / n_trials

# Simulated results (from Liu et al. 2023)
positions = [5, 25, 50, 75, 95]
for pct in positions:
    acc = max(0, 0.95 - 0.002 * abs(pct - 50))  # Simplified U-shaped curve
    print(f"Needle at {pct:2d}%: {acc:.0%} accuracy")

# Staff takeaway: always benchmark your actual model + context length
# The U-shape varies by model family (MHA > GQA for middle retention)

sections.title: Prompt Caching vs RAG vs Long Context
sections.description:
psychology Intuition: Three strategies for handling large contexts at scale. Prompt caching: precompute KV cache for a shared prefix (system prompt, few-shot examples) and reuse across queries. RAG: retrieve only the most relevant documents for each query. Long context: put everything in the context window. Each has a different cost-latency-quality tradeoff.

bar_chart Concrete numbers: Prompt caching saves 30-60% on decode time by avoiding recomputation of the shared prefix KV cache for chat sessions. RAG adds 50-200ms retrieval latency but reduces context to 2-5K tokens (vs 50-128K). Long context at 128K adds ~500ms to prefill time vs 2K context. For a chatbot with 10M conversations/day, prompt caching saves ~300K A100-hours/month (~$150K/month).

track_changes Decision Framework:

ScenarioStrategyCost per Query
<50K tokens, shared prefix across requestsPrompt Caching$0.001
>50K tokens or dynamic per-query contextRAG$0.003
Full document context required (legal, code review)Long Context$0.01-0.05
sections.codeBlock:
# Prompt caching: KV cache reuse computation
import hashlib, json

class PromptCache:
    """Simple KV cache manager for shared prefixes."""
    def __init__(self, model_backend):
        self.backend = model_backend
        self.cache = {}  # prefix_hash -> KV_cache

    def infer(self, system_prompt: str, user_query: str) -> str:
        prefix_hash = hashlib.md5(system_prompt.encode()).hexdigest()
        if prefix_hash in self.cache:
            # Reuse cached prefix KV, only compute for user_query tokens
            cached_kv = self.cache[prefix_hash]
            return self.backend.generate(cached_kv, user_query)
        # First time: compute full prefix + query
        full_kv, output = self.backend.generate_full(system_prompt + "\n" + user_query)
        # Cache only the prefix KV (system prompt tokens)
        self.cache[prefix_hash] = self.backend.extract_prefix_kv(full_kv, len(system_prompt))
        return output

# Cost comparison (assuming Llama 3 70B at $2/M tokens)
def cost_per_query(strategy, context_tokens, output_tokens, n_queries=1e7):
    prices = {"cache": 0.50, "rag": 1.50, "long": 8.00}  # $/M tokens relative
    caching_savings = 0.4 if strategy == "cache" else 0
    effective_tokens = context_tokens * (1 - caching_savings) + output_tokens
    per_query = effective_tokens * prices.get(strategy, 1.0) / 1e6
    print(f"{strategy:10s}: ${per_query:.4f}/query, ${per_query * n_queries / 1e6:.1f}M/month")

cost_per_query("cache", 50000, 200)
cost_per_query("rag", 3000, 200)
cost_per_query("long", 128000, 200)

sections.title: Production Prompt Management
sections.description:
psychology Intuition: Prompts in production are not static strings — they are code. They need version control, testing, monitoring, and rollback. At Staff level, treat prompt management with the same rigor as model weight management. A poorly-managed prompt can cause silent degradation that costs millions in lost revenue before it's detected.

bar_chart Concrete numbers: 20% of prompt issues are silent failures — the model returns valid-looking output that is semantically wrong (e.g., neutral sentiment classified as positive). Companies running A/B prompt tests typically see 5-15% metric variation between prompt variants. Without versioning, a prompt rollback takes hours instead of minutes. At 10M queries/day, even a 10 minutes rollback = ~7K bad responses.

track_changes Production Checklist: 1) Version control prompts in Git (separate from code, with semantic versioning). 2) A/B test every prompt change: serve 5% → 25% → 100%. 3) Monitor drift: track output distribution (response length, refusal rate, entity counts) over time. 4) CI/CD prompt pipeline: lint → validate on test set → canary → deploy. 5) Human-in-the-loop: sample 0.1% of outputs for manual review. 6) Prompt registry: metadata store with prompt hash, version, deployment date, performance metrics.

search Staff Engineer Question: "Product wants to change the system prompt from 'You are a helpful assistant' to 'You are a helpful assistant specializing in Python'. How do you roll this out?" Answer: 1) Create new prompt version v2.3.1 in the prompt registry. 2) Run offline validation against 500 test queries. 3) A/B test at 5% for 24 hours, comparing: response quality (human eval on 100 samples), latency, refusal rate, output distribution (does 'Python specialization' cause the model to refuse non-Python questions?). 4) If metrics pass thresholds, ramp to 25% for 48 hours. 5) Full rollout with monitoring for 1 week. 6) If issues found, rollback via prompt version switch in <1 minute.

sections.title: References & Further Reading
sections.description:
• Lost in the Middle: How Language Models Use Long Contexts (Liu et al., 2023) — The definitive study on positional bias in LLM context windows. Shows the U-shaped accuracy curve at 128K context.
  • DSPy: Compiling Declarative Language Model Calls into Self-Improving Pipelines (Khattab et al., 2024) — The DSPy framework for prompt optimization as program compilation.
  • Outlines: Guided Generation for LLMs — Constrained decoding that guarantees valid JSON/CSV/programming language output.
  • Scaling Datasets with Automatic Quality Estimation for LLM Training — Prompts as a quality lever; relevant to how prompt management intersects with training data quality.

details: Context Management Decision Flow: Budget (input + output < window) → Strategy selection (cache? RAG? long?) → Structure (important info first/last) → Monitor (positional bias, drift) → Iterate.
